The **cashew** is the nut of the cashew tree, *Anacardium occidentale*.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

Cashews are tan, crescent-shaped nuts.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) They have a very mild flavor, tender texture, and high fat content, which work together to give the perception of richness and creaminess.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:4-3)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Because they are so high in fat, cashews can spoil by going rancid.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) As such, they should be kept in an airtight container away from light and heat. They will last even longer when kept in the freezer.[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Cashews are popular, and their rich flavor means they are often eaten on their own, lightly salted, or sugared.[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4) Their richness and mild flavor lead to their use as dairy substitutes.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4) Cashew nuts also feature in Thai cuisine and Chinese cuisine,[[3]](#cite_note-:4-3) generally in whole form, and in Indian cuisine, often ground into sauces such as shahi korma or used as garnish in Indian sweets and desserts.[[3]](#cite_note-:4-3) In Malaysia, the young leaves are often eaten raw as salad or with sambal belacan (shrimp paste mixed with chili and lime). In Brazil, the cashew fruit juice is popular all across the country. Additionally, visitors to northeastern areas such as Fortaleza will often find cashew nut vendors selling the nuts for low cost, salted in a plastic bag upon purchase. In the Philippines, cashew is a known product of Antipolo, and is eaten with suman. Pampanga also has a sweet dessert called turrones de casuy which is cashew marzipan wrapped in white wafer.
